one_line_takeaway: A frozen-LLM multi-agent + trainable-MLP pipeline that decomposes crash-severity prediction by feature category and fuses per-category scores, improving Macro-F1/calibration over prompting baselines on two micromobility injury datasets—though its headline 'best-accuracy-everywhere' claim is contradicted by its own NEISS-Whole tables where classical Ordered-Logit still wins; not a security paper.
key_finding: TransportAgent outperforms prompting-based LLM baselines (k-shot, CoT, AutoGen) on both datasets and improves Macro-F1 / calibration on the higher-severity minority classes that single-agent LLMs and standalone MLPs under-predict. On CPSRMS its accuracy gains are clear and consistent across all three backbones. IMPORTANT CAVEAT grounded in the paper's own tables: on NEISS(Whole) the framework does NOT achieve the highest accuracy the abstract claims — with GPT-3.5-turbo and GPT-4o-mini the traditional Ordered-Logit (80.4%) and plain MLP (78.7-79.1%) beat TransportAgent (76.41% / 74.84%); TransportAgent only overtakes them on NEISS(Whole) accuracy with the LLaMA-3.3-70B backbone (85.78%). Its consistent win on NEISS is on Macro-F1 and on the class-balanced NEISS(Subset), not on raw NEISS(Whole) accuracy.
core_contribution: TransportAgent, a hybrid multi-agent LLM framework for predicting the severity level (ordinal injury/fatality outcome) of traffic crash incidents. The paper's OWN contribution is the pipeline architecture: (1) a Data Preprocessing Team with a Feature Selection Agent (filters attributes from a manually-annotated variable-name set) and a Conceptual Category Organizer (groups selected features into high-level conceptual categories such as demographics, environmental context, incident details); (2) a Severity Assessment Team where a Group Orchestrator assigns a dedicated LLM Evaluation Agent to each conceptual group, each agent independently reasoning over its structured + narrative inputs to emit a category-specific severity score s_k; and (3) an Integration Manager Module that feeds the vector of per-category scores into a supervised multilayer perceptron (MLP) trained with cross-entropy loss to fuse them into the final severity prediction. The stated novelty vs. prior LLM traffic work (CrashSage [31], MARBLE [30]) is coupling category-decomposed multi-agent reasoning with a supervised MLP fusion layer, keeping the LLM agents frozen and training only the MLP.
threat_model: None. This is not a security paper. Despite the cluster label 'Autonomous Vehicle Security', there is no adversary, attacker, threat, attack surface, or defense anywhere in the text. The 'severity' referred to is medical injury severity of crash victims (Incident/No-Injury through Death), not a security severity. No threat model is present and none is invented here.
limitations: Overclaim vs. own evidence: the abstract/Sec-F assert TransportAgent achieves 'the highest accuracy across all backbones and both datasets,' but Tables III and IV show Ologit (80.4%) and MLP/MLP+FS (78.7-79.1%) outrank TransportAgent's accuracy on NEISS(Whole) for the GPT-3.5-turbo and GPT-4o-mini backbones. Internal numeric inconsistency between narrative and tables: Sec-F text cites NEISS accuracies of 76.9% (LLaMA), 75.94% (GPT-4o-mini), and 63.21% (GPT-3.5) that match none of the NEISS(Whole)/NEISS(Subset) values in Tables V/IV/III. Small datasets: 1555 (CPSRMS), 1059 (NEISS-Whole), and only 397 (NEISS-Subset) test samples; results may not generalize. No comparison to the domain SOTA it cites: CrashSage [31] and MARBLE [30] are discussed as the closest LLM-based traffic-severity frameworks but are never run as baselines; baselines are limited to generic k-shot/CoT/AutoGen and classical ML (MLP, Ologit). Reliance on a manually-annotated attribute-name set for the Feature Selection Agent, and on a hand-defined cross-dataset severity mapping, limits automation/reproducibility. The standalone MLP collapses on CPSRMS (19.3% accuracy) but performs well on NEISS, indicating strong dataset-specific label-imbalance effects that complicate cross-dataset comparison; NEISS results are partly reported on a re-balanced subset. Scope is confined to filtered micromobility/roadway crash cases from injury-surveillance systems, not general traffic crashes.

datasets_used: CPSRMS (Consumer Product Safety Risk Management System, U.S., 2017-2023), filtered to transportation/roadway micromobility crash cases; test samples: 1555

NEISS (National Electronic Injury Surveillance System, U.S. hospital emergency departments), filtered to transportation crash cases; NEISS(Whole) test samples: 1059, NEISS(Subset, class-balanced) test samples: 397
Unified 4-level ordinal severity target (CPSRMS: 1 No-Injury, 2 Non-admission Medical Care, 3 Hospital Admission, 4 Death; NEISS: 1 Mild, 2 Moderate, 3 Severe, 4 Fatal)
quantitative_results: TransportAgent accuracy CPSRMS: 74.6% (GPT-3.5-turbo, Table III), 72.67% (GPT-4o-mini, Table IV), 73.31% (LLaMA-3.3-70B, Table V)
Baseline accuracy on CPSRMS (GPT-3.5, Table III): k-shot 55.31%, CoT 62.38%, AutoGen 66.56%, MLP 19.3%, MLP+feature-selection 19.3%, Ologit 64.6%

On NEISS(Whole) the classical baselines OUTPERFORM TransportAgent on accuracy for GPT-3.5/GPT-4o-mini backbones: Ologit 80.4%, MLP 78.7%, MLP+FS 79.1% vs TransportAgent 76.41%/74.84% (though TransportAgent leads on Macro-F1: 0.449/0.534 vs Ologit 0.223, MLP 0.312)
Train-test split robustness (CPSRMS, GPT-3.5, K=10 stratified 80/20): TransportAgent 70.69% +/- 1.76, CoT 63.22% +/- 1.97, k-shot 54.04% +/- 1.08

Structured-variable associations with severity (exploratory): CPSRMS strongest with Number-of-People-Involved (rho ~= 0.36) and Gender (V ~= 0.34); NEISS strongest with Primary-Injury-Body-Part (V ~= 0.29)
